titel: Enhanced protection for democracy and the independence of the courts.
html:
Sweden is currently facing the most serious security situation since World War II. Despite this, the Swedish democratic legal system remains strong and is firmly rooted in our fundamental laws. At the same time, developments in parts of the world demonstrate that political forces can undermine democracy and the rule of law in a short period. This often happens under the pretext that the measures are democratically legitimate, necessary, and in accordance with the will of the people. The government believes that this development indicates a need to make changes to the constitutional framework to strengthen the institutional protection of Sweden as a democratic legal state. The proposals in this document primarily aim to make it more difficult to amend the constitution and to strengthen the long-term independence of the courts and judges.

The proposed changes include a requirement that at least half of the members of the Riksdag (Swedish Parliament) must vote in favor of a motion to put a constitutional proposal on hold, and that a qualified majority of at least two-thirds of the members of the Riksdag must vote in favor of the second, confirmatory vote to adopt the proposal after a general election has been held. As a general rule, the decision to put a constitutional proposal on hold should be made no later than four months before election day. Furthermore, it is proposed that draft laws regarding fundamental rights and freedoms should be subject to review by the Council on Legislation (Lagrådet).

The government proposes changes to the constitutional provisions regarding the foundations of the state, clarifying that judicial activities are carried out by independent courts. The government also proposes changes to the constitution aimed at reducing the risk of political influence over the central court system.

Proposed Resolutions for the Riksdag

The government's proposals:

  1. The Riksdag adopts the government's proposal for a law amending the constitution.
  2. The Riksdag adopts the government's proposal for a law amending the Rules of Procedure for the Riksdag.

6.
tldr: The government wants to amend the constitution to make it more difficult to undermine democracy and to ensure that the courts can be completely independent from politicians.
lattlast:
Sweden faces an uncertain security situation in the world. The government is concerned that political forces might be able to change the rules of our democracy too quickly. Therefore, they want to amend the Instrument of Government, which is one of Sweden's most important laws.

The proposed change would require a larger number of votes in the Riksdag (Swedish Parliament) to amend the constitution. First, at least half of the members of the Riksdag must vote in favor of a proposal. Then, a new decision must be made after an election, and at that point, at least two-thirds of the members must vote in favor. This would prevent changes from being made hastily between elections.

The government also wants to protect the courts. Courts are responsible for judging legal cases, and they must be independent. This means that politicians should not be able to dictate how judges make their decisions. By changing the rules, the aim is to reduce the risk of politicians influencing the work of the courts.

Finally, it is proposed that the Council on Legislation (Lagrådet) should review all proposals that affect our most important fundamental rights and freedoms. The Council on Legislation is a group of experienced judges who review new laws to ensure they comply with Swedish regulations. The purpose is to create a stronger protection for all people living in Sweden.
